---
title: "Session 2 · Simple Linear Regression"
---

## Overview

This session introduces the workhorse of the entire course: the simple linear regression model. We learn how a single straight line summarizes the relationship between two variables, what the slope and intercept actually mean, and how to fit and interpret a model with R's `lm()`. Everything that follows --- dummies, ANCOVA, and multiple predictors --- is a generalization of the ideas we establish here.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- State the simple linear regression model and define its slope and intercept.
- Fit a simple linear regression in R using the `lm()` function.
- Interpret the estimated slope and intercept in the context of the data.
- Compute and interpret fitted values and residuals.
- Distinguish between the population model and its sample estimates.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 1: Simple Linear Regression](/notes/chapters/01-simple-linear-regression.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Lab:** [Lab 2: Simple Linear Regression](/labs/labs/lab02-simple-regression.html){target="_blank"}

## Key concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Simple linear regression** | A model relating an outcome to a single predictor through a straight line. |
| **Slope** | The expected change in the outcome for a one-unit increase in the predictor. |
| **Intercept** | The expected value of the outcome when the predictor equals zero. |
| **Fitted value** | The model's predicted outcome for a given value of the predictor. |
| **Residual** | The difference between an observed outcome and its fitted value. |
| **Least squares** | The estimation method that chooses the line minimizing the sum of squared residuals. |
| **`lm()`** | R's function for fitting linear models. |
